title: Build Links That Explain Where They Go
summary: A link combines an understandable accessible name with a URL. Absolute URLs identify complete locations; relative URLs preserve project relationships; fragment URLs target IDs in a document; and mailto or tel schemes offer appropriate communication actions. Link purpose must remain clear outside its surrounding sentence. Predict, inspect, explain, and apply the contract in a cumulative public-information build.
objectives: Explain html links urls with source and browser evidence.
Apply html links urls without discarding earlier document work.
Diagnose the named misconception in a changed public-service scenario.

steps.title: Predict the multi-channel help directory failure · theory
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 1: Which claim should guide the first multi-channel help directory decision before the page is opened? Work context: multi-channel help directory.
steps.why: Build Links That Explain Where They Go requires this decision because the learner must distinguish a specification-defined relationship from a browser-recovered appearance. This is checkpoint 1 in the multi-channel help directory.

options.text: Multi-channel help directory case 1: every internal project link needs the author’s full local file-system path.
Multi-channel help directory case 1: the href must use the right URL form and the link text should identify the destination or action.
Multi-channel help directory case 1: a fragment link targets a heading by its visible text without requiring an id.

steps.title: Reconstruct the multi-channel help directory workflow · theory
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 2: Put the four html links urls decisions into a defensible working order. Work context: multi-channel help directory.
steps.why: Build Links That Explain Where They Go depends on causal ordering because the learner must distinguish a specification-defined relationship from a browser-recovered appearance. This is checkpoint 2 in the multi-channel help directory.

content.text: Reconstruct the multi-channel help directory sequence by dependency, not by how familiar each term looks.

options.text: multi-channel help directory phase 2.1: identify the destination and user intent
multi-channel help directory phase 2.2: choose absolute, relative, fragment, email, or telephone URL syntax
multi-channel help directory phase 2.3: write link text that names the destination or action
multi-channel help directory phase 2.4: activate the link and verify the resulting location

Multi-channel help directory case 5: add presentation until the multi-channel help directory source relationship no longer matters.

Multi-channel help directory case 6: identify the purpose, apply the defined html links urls contract, and verify the result with more than appearance.
Multi-channel help directory case 6: use the longest available element or attribute name because it must be more semantic.

steps.title: Defend the multi-channel help directory repair · theory
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 7: Explain why “Click here is always sufficient link text because users read every surrounding sentence first.” is unreliable and name the evidence that would verify the repair. Work context: multi-channel help directory.
steps.why: Build Links That Explain Where They Go uses explanation to expose guesses that a passing visual result could hide. This is checkpoint 7 in the multi-channel help directory.
